title: Snort IDS Detection
detectionType: Network IDS/IPS / Meraki Snort — egress malware/C2 signature
description: The Meraki security appliance's Snort engine fired an intrusion signature on traffic from an internal endpoint to an external destination. These alerts are scoped to egress threat signatures only — malware command-and-control, botnet, and similar outbound-threat categories — with inbound probing, PUA, and policy signatures deliberately suppressed. Every alert therefore means an internal host may be communicating with malicious infrastructure. The investigation's central challenge is that Meraki reports a network-level source (often a NAT'd internal IP, MAC, or client name behind the appliance): the analyst must first identify the actual host, then attempt process-level attribution in the endpoint platform (CrowdStrike). A distinct path handles hosts that are NOT in CrowdStrike, where the security team lacks endpoint access and must engage the client's technician, while still acting at the network layer.

intake.title: Intake and capture the Snort event
intake.instructions:
Confirm this is a new alert (not a duplicate) and capture everything the IDS event provides.

- Record the Snort signature: rule name/ID, category (malware CnC, botnet, etc.), and the message text.
- Record the flow: internal source IP (as seen by the appliance), external destination IP/port/hostname, and protocol.
- Note whether the appliance action was DETECT (alerted only) or PREVENT (blocked) — this changes urgency and whether the connection actually completed.
- Record the event timestamp(s) and whether the signature fired once or repeatedly (single hit vs. sustained/periodic).
- Note which Meraki network/appliance and, if applicable, which client/tenant the alert belongs to.

evidence.label: Flow: internal src IP → dest IP/host/port/proto

characterize.title: Characterize the signature and destination
characterize.instructions:
Establish how credible the threat indicator is before spending effort on host hunting — but note these are already scoped to egress threat categories, so the default posture is suspicion.

- Enrich the external destination (IP and hostname) in a threat-intel capability: known C2, malware distribution, botnet, sinkhole, or clean/miscategorized.
- Assess the signature's fidelity: is this rule specific to a known malware family/C2 protocol, or a broader heuristic that can trip on legitimate traffic?
- Determine whether the destination is a known-good service that the signature misfires on (e.g. a legitimate CDN, cloud, or app that resembles C2 patterns).
- Note whether the same destination or signature is firing for multiple internal hosts (campaign/worm indicator) or just one.

evidence.label: Signature fidelity (specific family vs. broad heuristic)
One host or many hitting this dest/signature?

identify-host.title: Identify the actual host from Meraki data
identify-host.instructions:
The appliance reports a network-level source. Resolve it to a specific physical host using everything Meraki knows.

- In the Meraki client/flow data, map the internal source IP at the event timestamp to a client entry: hostname, MAC address, and manufacturer (OUI often reveals device type).
- Capture connection context: wired switchport or wireless SSID, VLAN, and the assigned/DHCP name, to physically locate the device.
- Review the client's other flows around the event for corroborating malicious traffic or additional destinations.
- Beware NAT and DHCP churn: confirm the IP-to-client mapping was valid at the exact event time, not just now. Note the user associated with the device if Meraki or directory data reveals it.
- Record the strongest device fingerprint you can assemble (hostname + MAC + location + user).

evidence.label: Meraki client match (hostname / MAC / OUI / manufacturer)
Connection context (switchport / SSID / VLAN / DHCP name)
Other suspicious flows from same client

host-identified?.title: Were you able to identify a specific host?
host-identified?.instructions: Decide whether Meraki data pinned the traffic to a specific device, or whether the source remains ambiguous (stale mapping, no client record, transient/guest device).

options.label: No — cannot resolve to a specific device
options.note: Act at network layer; pursue identification.

in-edr?.title: Is the identified host managed in the endpoint platform (CrowdStrike)?
in-edr?.instructions: This determines whether the security team can perform process-level attribution directly, or must engage the client's technician because it lacks endpoint access to that device.

options.label: No — host not in CrowdStrike (no security-team access)

edr-investigate.title: Investigate the host in CrowdStrike
edr-investigate.instructions:
The host is managed. Pivot from the network flow to process-level attribution around the Snort event timestamps.

- Locate the host in the endpoint platform and search its network telemetry for connections to the flagged destination IP/host/port at the event time.
- Identify the initiating process for that connection: a browser or known application (possible false positive / user activity), or an unexpected binary (script host, LOLBin, unsigned/renamed executable, temp/user-path) indicating malware the network caught but prevention missed.
- For any suspicious process, capture path, signature, parent, reputation, persistence, and whether the endpoint platform raised related (possibly lower-severity or suppressed) detections.
- Determine whether the traffic is autonomous (periodic C2 beaconing) or tied to user action, and whether connections actually succeeded (especially if the appliance was DETECT-only and the connection completed).

evidence.label: Matching connection found in EDR telemetry?
Autonomous beaconing vs. user-driven; connection success

edr-verdict?.title: What does the endpoint investigation conclude?
edr-verdict?.instructions: A suspicious process or autonomous beaconing to credible malicious infrastructure confirms infection. A legitimate process reaching a destination the signature misfires on points to a false positive. If evidence is mixed or the connection is unclear, treat as suspicious and confirm.

deep-analysis.title: Deepen the endpoint analysis
deep-analysis.instructions:
The initial endpoint review was inconclusive. Collect more before deciding.

- Pull the suspect binary for reputation/sandbox analysis and examine its full behavior (network, persistence, injection, file/registry changes).
- Extend the timeline: earlier and later activity on the host, other destinations contacted, and any staging or discovery behavior.
- Correlate with identity/authentication logs for the associated user if account involvement is possible.
- Where feasible, confirm the activity with the user (did they run/download anything matching the timeframe).

deep-verdict?.title: After deeper analysis, is this malicious?
deep-verdict?.instructions: Resolve the inconclusive case. Malicious behavior confirmed = infection. Legitimate explanation established = false positive.

not-in-edr.title: Host not in CrowdStrike — engage the client technician and act at the network layer
not-in-edr.instructions:
The security team has no endpoint access to this device, so process attribution must be done by the client's technician. Do not treat this as a dead end — act at the network layer immediately and hand off a precise hunt package.

- Take proportionate network-layer protective action now: block/deny the malicious destination on the Meraki appliance (and switch the signature to PREVENT if it was DETECT-only) so the host is denied C2 while it is tracked down.
- Assemble a hunt package for the client's technician using the fingerprint from Meraki: hostname, MAC, switchport/SSID/VLAN, user, event time, destination, and signature — enough to physically locate the device.
- Instruct the technician on what to look for once at the host: unexpected running processes/services making the connection, recently installed software, scheduled tasks/persistence, and recent downloads or email attachments around the event time; and to preserve evidence rather than immediately wiping.
- Request that the technician report findings back, and (if policy allows) that the endpoint be enrolled in the endpoint platform to enable direct investigation.

evidence.label: Hunt package sent to technician (fingerprint + what to check)

tech-findings?.title: What did the client technician find at the host?
tech-findings?.instructions: Route on the technician's report. Confirmed malicious artifacts = infection to escalate. A clear benign explanation = false positive. No usable finding (device gone, wiped, unreachable, or technician cannot determine) = unresolved.

unresolved-host.title: Unresolved host — network protection and coverage note
unresolved-host.instructions:
The specific host could not be identified or confirmed. Do not simply close — the alert may reflect a real infection you cannot yet see.

- Ensure the malicious destination is blocked/denied on the Meraki appliance so any affected host is denied C2 while attribution continues.
- Document everything known about the source (internal IP, MAC/OUI if any, switchport/SSID/VLAN, tenant, time window) and the reason attribution failed (NAT/DHCP churn, guest/transient device, no client record, no endpoint sensor coverage).
- File the gap as a coverage-improvement item (logging, sensor deployment, network segmentation/visibility).
- Decide whether the malicious-destination evidence is strong enough to escalate for broader hunting, or can be monitored with defined re-open triggers (repeat signature hits, new hosts hitting the same destination).

evidence.label: What is known about the source & why attribution failed

unresolved-verdict?.title: Is the destination evidence strong enough to escalate?
unresolved-verdict?.instructions: Credible contact with known-bad C2/malware infrastructure may justify escalation for broader hunting even without a confirmed host; weak or ambiguous evidence can be blocked, monitored, and closed with a coverage note.

options.label: Strong — credible C2/malware infrastructure contacted
options.note: Escalate for hunting despite no host.

contain-host.title: Contain the host and infrastructure
contain-host.instructions:
Confirmed malicious C2/infection. Contain before scoping and handoff.

- Isolate the affected host: network-contain it in the endpoint platform if managed, or use network-level isolation on the appliance/switch (block, quarantine VLAN, disable port) if it is not.
- Block/sinkhole the malicious destination(s) across the environment and set the signature to PREVENT if it was DETECT-only.
- On a managed host, block the payload by hash, kill the offending process, and remove persistence.
- Record every containment action with timestamps.

evidence.label: Destination block/sinkhole; signature set to PREVENT
Hash block / process kill / persistence removal (if managed)

scope.title: Scope across the environment
scope.instructions:
Determine how far the threat reached before handoff.

- Query the Meraki flow/IDS data across networks for any OTHER internal hosts contacting the same destination or tripping the same signature.
- On the endpoint platform, hunt the payload hash and related indicators across all managed hosts.
- Build a timeline from first contact to latest activity and identify lateral movement or additional affected hosts/accounts (review identity logs for suspicious auth tied to affected users).
- Assess what data/systems the affected host(s) could reach and whether exfiltration occurred.

escalate.title: Escalate to incident response
escalate.instructions:
Confirmed (or strongly suspected) malicious egress activity with initial containment and scope established. Hand off with a complete package.

- Ensure signature/flow details, destination enrichment, host identification, process/payload findings (or technician report), timeline, affected hosts/accounts, and containment actions are all recorded above.
- Assign incident severity per your matrix and notify the on-call IR owner.
- Confirm volatile evidence was preserved and Meraki/EDR/identity logs retained before their retention windows expire.
- If the appliance was DETECT-only and the connection completed, or if endpoint prevention missed the payload, note the gap for post-incident review.
- Transfer ownership; eradication and recovery proceed under the IR playbook.
escalate.summary: Snort egress signature confirmed as malicious C2/infection; host contained (or network-isolated), scoped via Meraki + EDR, and escalated to IR.

close-fp.title: Close — false positive
close-fp.instructions:
The signature fired on legitimate traffic: a benign process reaching a clean or miscategorized destination, or a broad heuristic misfiring on a legitimate service.

- Document the benign explanation and the confirming evidence (legitimate process, clean destination enrichment, user confirmation, or technician finding).
- Propose scoped tuning: refine or suppress the specific signature for the confirmed-benign destination without weakening genuine egress-threat coverage.
- Note any signature that is systematically over-firing for detection engineering.
close-fp.summary: Snort egress signature determined a false positive (legitimate traffic / miscategorized destination); scoped tuning proposed.

close-unresolved.title: Close — unattributed, network protection in place
close-unresolved.instructions:
The host could not be identified or confirmed, and the destination evidence did not warrant full escalation, but protective steps are in place.

- Confirm the malicious/suspect destination is blocked on the appliance and the signature is at PREVENT.
- Record the attribution failure and the coverage-improvement item (visibility, sensor coverage, logging, segmentation).
- Define explicit re-open triggers: repeat signature hits, the same destination reappearing, or new hosts contacting it.
close-unresolved.summary: Snort egress alert could not be attributed to a confirmed host; destination blocked at the appliance and a visibility-gap coverage item filed. Closed with monitoring and re-open triggers.
